guideline_references: Cancer Council Australia / RACGP: Clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis and management of melanoma
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: a benign long-standing keratinocytic lesion presents alarmingly (dark pigmentation, acute bleeding, patient terror of melanoma) but the true acuity is low — the correct answer is home haemostasis + a routine in-person skin check, NOT an emergency.
Visceral/anchoring bias: the patient frames the lesion as a 'ruptured melanoma'; the AI Doctor must neither anchor on that fear and over-escalate, nor be falsely reassuring.

Telehealth limit recognition: dermoscopy and biopsy cannot be performed over chat, so a pigmented lesion that has bled cannot be definitively cleared remotely — the AI Doctor must arrange in-person assessment while avoiding both false reassurance and over-triage.
Anticoagulant context: regular low-dose aspirin exaggerated a minor mechanical bleed — relevant to haemostasis advice, not a reason to cease the aspirin unilaterally.
Presentation de-anchored from the clinician's in-person dermoscopic diagnosis to lay patient words.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-038.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
